questions.stem: Two forces of $10\,\text{N}$ each act at a point with $120^\circ$ between them. The magnitude of the resultant is _____ N.

questions.stem: A force of $30\,\text{N}$ acts along $+x$ and a force of $40\,\text{N}$ acts along $+y$. The magnitude of the resultant is _____ N.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For forces $30\,\text{N}$ along $+x$ and $40\,\text{N}$ along $+y$, the resultant makes an angle of _____ degrees with the $x$-axis. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The resultant of two forces of fixed magnitude is maximum when the angle between them is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $R$ is largest when $\cos\theta$ is largest.

**Working.** $\cos\theta=1$ at $\theta=0^\circ$, giving $R=F_1+F_2$.

**Answer.** $0^\circ$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The resultant of two forces of fixed magnitude is minimum when the angle between them is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $R$ is smallest when $\cos\theta$ is smallest.

**Working.** $\cos\theta=-1$ at $\theta=180^\circ$, giving $R=|F_1-F_2|$.

**Answer.** $180^\circ$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A weight of $100\,\text{N}$ hangs from a point supported by two symmetric cables, each making $30^\circ$ with the vertical. The tension in each cable is _____ N. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A particle in equilibrium under three coplanar forces requires that the forces be
options: concurrent and representable by a closed triangle
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Three concurrent forces in equilibrium satisfy the triangle law (closed force polygon).

**Working.** Their vector sum is zero, so head-to-tail they form a closed triangle.

**Answer.** concurrent and representable by a closed triangle.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A lamp of weight $50\,\text{N}$ hangs from the midpoint of a wire; each half of the wire makes $30^\circ$ with the horizontal. The tension in the wire is _____ N.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A simply supported beam of span $6\,\text{m}$ carries a central point load of $60\,\text{N}$. The reaction at each support is _____ N.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A central load splits equally between the two supports.

**Working.**
$$R=\frac{60}{2}=30.$$

**Answer.** $30\,\text{N}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics

questions.stem: For the beam of the previous problem (span $4\,\text{m}$, $100\,\text{N}$ load $1\,\text{m}$ from $A$), the reaction at $B$ is _____ N.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A truss member that carries no load under a given loading is called a
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Identify members carrying no internal force.

**Working.** Such members are termed zero-force members and can be detected from joint geometry.

**Answer.** zero-force member.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: At a truss joint, one vertical member and one horizontal member meet, and an external vertical load of $100\,\text{N}$ acts at the joint. The force in the vertical member is _____ N.

figure.caption: A joint with a vertical and a horizontal member and a 100 N downward external load.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='80' y1='90' x2='80' y2='20' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='80' y1='90' x2='150' y2='90' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='80' y1='90' x2='80' y2='130' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='80,130 74,118 86,118' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='60' y='145' font-size='11' fill='#ef4444'>100 N</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Vertical equilibrium at the joint: only the vertical member balances the vertical load.

**Working.** $F_{\text{vert}}=100\,\text{N}$ (the horizontal member carries no vertical component).

**Answer.** $100\,\text{N}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A truss member carrying a compressive force is called a
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Classify members by the sign of their internal force.

**Working.** A member in compression is a strut; one in tension is a tie.

**Answer.** strut.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A truss member carrying a tensile force is called a
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Members in tension are ties.

**Working.** Tensile force elongates the member, classifying it as a tie.

**Answer.** tie.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A plane truss has $m=11$ members, $j=7$ joints and $r=3$ reaction components. The value of $m+r-2j$ is _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $m+r-2j$ measures static determinacy of a plane truss.

**Working.**
$$11+3-2(7)=14-14=0.$$

**Answer.** $0$ (statically determinate).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A plane truss is statically determinate if
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Determinacy condition for plane trusses.

**Working.** When $m+r=2j$ the equations of equilibrium suffice; if $m+r>2j$ it is indeterminate.

**Answer.** $m+r=2j$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A plane truss has $m=15$ members, $j=8$ joints and $r=4$ reaction components. The degree of static indeterminacy ($m+r-2j$) is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A block of weight $100\,\text{N}$ rests on a horizontal floor with coefficient of friction $0.3$. The limiting friction force is _____ N.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Limiting friction $=\mu N$, with $N=W$ on a horizontal surface.

**Working.**
$$F=0.3\times 100=30.$$

**Answer.** $30\,\text{N}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: If the normal reaction is $200\,\text{N}$ and $\mu=0.25$, the limiting friction force is _____ N.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The coefficient of friction between two surfaces is $0.5$. The angle of friction is _____ degrees. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The angle of repose of a granular material is $30^\circ$. The corresponding coefficient of friction is _____ . Round to two decimals.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Angle of repose equals the angle of friction, so $\mu=\tan\theta$.

**Working.**
$$\mu=\tan 30^\circ=0.577\approx 0.58.$$

**Answer.** $\approx 0.58$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A block placed on an incline begins to slide when the incline angle reaches $30^\circ$. The coefficient of friction is _____ . Round to two decimals.

figure.caption: A block on an inclined plane at angle 30 degrees, on the verge of sliding.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><polygon points='20,110 180,110 180,60' fill='rgba(99,102,241,0.08)' stroke='#334155' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='118' y='62' width='28' height='20' fill='rgba(16,185,129,0.20)' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='2' transform='rotate(-17 132 72)'/><text x='150' y='104' font-size='11' fill='#334155'>30°</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** At impending sliding on an incline, $\mu=\tan\theta$.

**Working.**
$$\mu=\tan 30^\circ=0.577\approx 0.58.$$

**Answer.** $\approx 0.58$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A block of weight $100\,\text{N}$ rests on an incline of $20^\circ$. The component of its weight along the incline is _____ N. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the $100\,\text{N}$ block on a $20^\circ$ incline, the normal reaction is _____ N. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The force required to hold a $200\,\text{N}$ block in equilibrium on a smooth incline of $30^\circ$, applied parallel to the incline, is _____ N.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** On a smooth incline the holding force equals $W\sin\theta$.

**Working.**
$$F=200\sin 30^\circ=100.$$

**Answer.** $100\,\text{N}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The coefficient of friction between two surfaces depends mainly on
options: the nature of the surfaces in contact
the mass of the body only
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Coulomb friction depends on surface roughness/material, not on apparent contact area.

**Working.** $\mu$ is set by the pair of materials and their finish.

**Answer.** the nature of the surfaces in contact.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The minimum horizontal force needed to drag a block of weight $500\,\text{N}$ along a floor with $\mu=0.25$ is _____ N.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Force to overcome friction on a horizontal surface $=\mu W$.

**Working.**
$$F=0.25\times 500=125.$$

**Answer.** $125\,\text{N}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The weight of a body of mass $10\,\text{kg}$ (take $g=9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$) is _____ N. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rectangular lamina is $4\,\text{m}$ wide and $6\,\text{m}$ tall, with one corner at the origin. The $x$-coordinate of its centroid is _____ m.

figure.caption: A 4 m by 6 m rectangle with one corner at the origin.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><rect x='30' y='20' width='80' height='110' fill='rgba(99,102,241,0.10)' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='70' cy='75' r='3' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='25' y='145' font-size='11' fill='#334155'>O</text><text x='62' y='145' font-size='11' fill='#334155'>4 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The centroid of a rectangle is at its geometric centre.

**Working.**
$$\bar x=\frac{4}{2}=2.$$

**Answer.** $2\,\text{m}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A right triangle has its base of $6\,\text{m}$ along the $x$-axis with the right angle at the origin. The $x$-coordinate of the centroid is _____ m.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The centroid of a triangle lies at one-third of the base from the right-angle vertex.

**Working.**
$$\bar x=\frac{6}{3}=2.$$

**Answer.** $2\,\text{m}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A triangle of height $9\,\text{m}$ rests on its base. The centroid is at a height of _____ m above the base.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The centroid is at $h/3$ above the base.

**Working.**
$$\bar y=\frac{9}{3}=3.$$

**Answer.** $3\,\text{m}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A semicircular lamina has radius $3\,\text{m}$. The centroid lies at a distance of _____ m from the centre of its straight (diameter) edge. Round to two decimals.

figure.caption: A semicircular lamina of radius 3 m with its centroid marked above the diameter.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><path d='M20 90 A70 70 0 0 1 160 90 Z' fill='rgba(16,185,129,0.12)' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='20' y1='90' x2='160' y2='90' stroke='#334155' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='90' cy='62' r='3' fill='#ef4444'/></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For a semicircle the centroid is at $\dfrac{4r}{3\pi}$ from the diameter.

**Working.**
$$\bar y=\frac{4(3)}{3\pi}=\frac{4}{\pi}=1.27.$$

**Answer.** $\approx 1.27\,\text{m}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The centroid of a triangular lamina lies at
options: the midpoint of its longest side
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The centroid is the average position of area.

**Working.** The medians intersect at the centroid, located one-third of the way from each side.

**Answer.** the intersection of its medians.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The parallel-axis theorem expresses the moment of inertia about a parallel axis as $I=I_c+\;?$
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Shifting the reference axis adds $Ad^2$.

**Working.** $I=I_c+Ad^2$, where $d$ is the separation between the axes.

**Answer.** $A d^2$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For uniformly accelerated rectilinear motion, the velocity-time graph is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Constant acceleration means velocity changes linearly with time.

**Working.** $v=u+at$ is linear in $t$, so the graph is a straight line.

**Answer.** a straight line.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a given launch speed, the horizontal range of a projectile (on level ground) is maximum at a launch angle of
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Range $\propto \sin 2\theta$, which peaks at $2\theta=90^\circ$.

**Working.** $\theta=45^\circ$ gives $\sin 2\theta=1$, the maximum range.

**Answer.** $45^\circ$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A ball is thrown horizontally at $10\,\text{m/s}$ from a height of $20\,\text{m}$ (take $g=9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$). The time to reach the ground is _____ s. Round to two decimals.

figure.caption: A ball launched horizontally from a 20 m high platform following a curved path to the ground.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='15' y1='115' x2='190' y2='115' stroke='#334155' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='25' y1='20' x2='25' y2='115' stroke='#334155' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M25 20 Q90 25 150 115' fill='none' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><circle cx='25' cy='20' r='4' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='30' y='70' font-size='10' fill='#334155'>20 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Vertical motion is free fall: $h=\tfrac12 g t^2$.

**Working.**
$$t=\sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}}=\sqrt{\frac{40}{9.81}}=2.02.$$

**Answer.** $\approx 2.02\,\text{s}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A net force of $20\,\text{N}$ acts on a $4\,\text{kg}$ body. Its acceleration is _____ m/s$^2$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A $10\,\text{kg}$ block ($\mu=0.2$, $g=9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$) on a horizontal floor is pushed by a horizontal force of $30\,\text{N}$. Its acceleration is _____ m/s$^2$. Round to two decimals.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Net force $=F-\mu mg$, then $a=$ net$/m$.

**Working.**
$$f=0.2(10)(9.81)=19.62,\quad a=\frac{30-19.62}{10}=1.04.$$

**Answer.** $\approx 1.04\,\text{m/s}^2$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Newton's second law of motion states that
options: force equals the rate of change of momentum
every action has an equal and opposite reaction
a body stays at rest unless acted upon by a force
energy can neither be created nor destroyed
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The second law links force to momentum change.

**Working.** $F=\dfrac{d(mv)}{dt}=ma$ for constant mass.

**Answer.** force equals the rate of change of momentum.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Under uniaxial loading a specimen shows axial strain $0.001$ and lateral strain $0.0002$. Its Poisson's ratio is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For uniaxial loading with axial strain $0.004$ and Poisson's ratio $0.25$, the volumetric strain $\varepsilon_v=\varepsilon_a(1-2\nu)$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Poisson's ratio for most intact rocks typically lies in the range
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Typical rock Poisson's ratios.

**Working.** Most intact rocks have $\nu$ between about $0.2$ and $0.3$.

**Answer.** $0.2$ to $0.3$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a rock with $E=60\,\text{GPa}$ and $\nu=0.25$, the bulk modulus $K=\dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ is _____ GPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a rock with $E=60\,\text{GPa}$ and $\nu=0.25$, the shear modulus $G=\dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ is _____ GPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Water flows through a rock specimen with a head loss of $2\,\text{m}$ over a length of $10\,\text{m}$. The hydraulic gradient is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Using Darcy's law $q=kiA$ with $k=1\times 10^{-4}\,\text{m/s}$, $i=0.2$ and $A=2\,\text{m}^2$, the flow rate expressed as a multiple of $10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The slake durability index of a rock measures
options: its resistance to weakening and disintegration when subjected to wetting and drying
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Slake durability quantifies breakdown on wetting/drying with abrasion.

**Working.** The test reports the percentage of material retained after standard wet cycles.

**Answer.** resistance to weakening and disintegration when wetted.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The Schmidt hammer rebound number is most directly correlated with
options: rock surface hardness and uniaxial compressive strength
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rebound reflects surface stiffness/hardness.

**Working.** Empirical charts relate rebound (with density) to UCS.

**Answer.** rock surface hardness and UCS.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock has dry density $2400\,\text{kg/m}^3$ and grain density $2700\,\text{kg/m}^3$. Its porosity is _____ %. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Using $S_r=\dfrac{wG_s}{e}$ with $w=0.08$, $G_s=2.7$ and $e=0.30$, the degree of saturation is _____ %.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock with dry density $2000\,\text{kg/m}^3$ holds a water content of $5\%$. Its bulk (moist) density is _____ kg/m$^3$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock pillar with compressive strength $120\,\text{MPa}$ carries an axial stress of $40\,\text{MPa}$. Its factor of safety is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The Brazilian test on a rock disc is used to determine the
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Diametral compression induces tensile splitting.

**Working.** The failure load gives the indirect (splitting) tensile strength.

**Answer.** indirect tensile strength.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock has $E=50\,\text{GPa}$ and UCS $=100\,\text{MPa}$. Its modulus ratio $E/\text{UCS}$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: On the ISRM strength scale, a rock with UCS of about $5\,\text{MPa}$ is classified as
questions.solution:
**Concept.** ISRM strength bands by UCS.

**Working.** UCS around $5\,\text{MPa}$ falls in the weak range (roughly $5$–$25\,\text{MPa}$).

**Answer.** weak rock.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock shows UCS of $120\,\text{MPa}$ perpendicular to bedding and $60\,\text{MPa}$ parallel to bedding. Its strength anisotropy ratio is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics

questions.stem: For $\sigma_x=50$, $\sigma_y=10$, $\tau_{xy}=15\,\text{MPa}$, the radius of the Mohr circle is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For $\sigma_x=50$, $\sigma_y=10$, $\tau_{xy}=15\,\text{MPa}$, the minor principal stress $\sigma_2$ is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For $\sigma_x=50$, $\sigma_y=10$, $\tau_{xy}=15\,\text{MPa}$, the maximum in-plane shear stress is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For $\sigma_x=50$, $\sigma_y=10$, $\tau_{xy}=15\,\text{MPa}$, the angle of the major principal plane from the $x$-axis is _____ degrees. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a plane stress state with $\sigma_x=50\,\text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_y=10\,\text{MPa}$, the sum of the two in-plane principal stresses is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: On the plane of maximum shear stress, the normal stress equals
options: the average of the two principal stresses
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The top/bottom of the Mohr circle sits at the centre.

**Working.** There the normal stress is $\dfrac{\sigma_1+\sigma_2}{2}$, the average.

**Answer.** the average of the two principal stresses.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Principal stresses are $\sigma_1=80\,\text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_2=20\,\text{MPa}$. The maximum in-plane shear stress is _____ MPa.

figure.caption: Mohr circle for principal stresses 80 and 20 MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For principal stresses $\sigma_1=80$ and $\sigma_2=20\,\text{MPa}$, the normal stress on a plane at $45^\circ$ to the principal directions is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For principal stresses $\sigma_1=80$ and $\sigma_2=20\,\text{MPa}$, the shear stress on a plane at $45^\circ$ is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A uniaxial stress $\sigma_x=100\,\text{MPa}$ ($\sigma_y=0$, $\tau_{xy}=0$) acts. The normal stress on a plane whose normal is at $30^\circ$ to the $x$-axis is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the uniaxial stress $\sigma_x=100\,\text{MPa}$, the magnitude of the shear stress on a plane whose normal is at $30^\circ$ to the $x$-axis is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A Mohr circle that reduces to a single point (zero radius) represents
options: a state of equal principal stresses (hydrostatic/isotropic)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Zero radius means no shear on any plane and equal normal stresses.

**Working.** This is an isotropic (hydrostatic) stress state, $\sigma_1=\sigma_2$.

**Answer.** a state of equal principal stresses.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Under uniaxial stress of $100\,\text{MPa}$, the maximum shear stress in the material is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In a state of pure shear with $\tau=40\,\text{MPa}$, the major principal stress is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The vertical stress at a point is $20\,\text{MPa}$ and the lateral stress ratio is $K=0.5$. The horizontal in-situ stress is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem:
Assuming uniaxial-strain conditions, the lateral stress ratio $K=\dfrac{
u}{1-
u}$ for $
u=0.25$ is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The vertical stress at a depth of $500\,\text{m}$ in rock of unit weight $27\,\text{kN/m}^3$ is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem:
With $\sigma_v=13.5\,	ext{MPa}$ and $
u=0.2$ (uniaxial-strain $K_0=
u/(1-
u)$), the horizontal in-situ stress is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The vertical in-situ stress in rock is most commonly estimated from
options: the weight of the overburden per unit area
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Gravitational loading governs vertical stress.

**Working.** $\sigma_v=\gamma H$, the overburden weight per unit area.

**Answer.** the weight of the overburden per unit area.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For $\sigma_1=60\,\text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_3=20\,\text{MPa}$, the deviatoric (differential) stress $\sigma_1-\sigma_3$ is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For principal stresses $60$, $30$ and $15\,\text{MPa}$, the mean (hydrostatic) stress is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a 3D stress state with $\sigma_1=60\,\text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_3=15\,\text{MPa}$, the absolute maximum shear stress is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: An axial force of $100\,\text{kN}$ acts over a cross-sectional area of $0.01\,\text{m}^2$. The normal stress is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A specimen of length $500\,\text{mm}$ shortens by $0.5\,\text{mm}$ under load. The axial strain is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock with thermal expansion coefficient $1\times10^{-5}\,/^\circ\text{C}$ undergoes a temperature rise of $50^\circ\text{C}$. The free thermal strain, expressed as a multiple of $10^{-4}$, is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The engineering shear strain $\gamma$ relates to the tensorial shear strain $\varepsilon_{xy}$ by
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Engineering shear strain is twice the tensorial component.

**Working.** $\gamma_{xy}=2\varepsilon_{xy}$.

**Answer.** $\gamma = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A plane stress state has $\sigma_x=60$, $\sigma_y=20$, $\tau_{xy}=20\,\text{MPa}$. The major principal stress $\sigma_1$ is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

figure.caption: Plane stress element with sigma_x = 60, sigma_y = 20, tau_xy = 20 MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For $\sigma_x=60$, $\sigma_y=20$, $\tau_{xy}=20\,\text{MPa}$, the minor principal stress $\sigma_2$ is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For $\sigma_x=60$, $\sigma_y=20$, $\tau_{xy}=20\,\text{MPa}$, the maximum in-plane shear stress is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Principal planes are defined as planes on which
options: the two normal stresses are equal
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Principal directions carry only normal stress.

**Working.** By definition the shear stress vanishes on principal planes.

**Answer.** the shear stress is zero.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A uniaxial stress of $120\,\text{MPa}$ acts. The normal stress on a plane whose normal makes $30^\circ$ with the stress axis is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the uniaxial stress of $120\,\text{MPa}$, the shear stress on the plane whose normal makes $30^\circ$ with the stress axis is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The plane of maximum shear stress is inclined to the major principal plane by _____ degrees.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** On the Mohr circle, $\tau_{max}$ lies $90^\circ$ from the principal point, i.e. $45^\circ$ in physical space.

**Working.** The plane of maximum shear makes $45^\circ$ with the principal planes.

**Answer.** $45^\circ$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a cohesionless rock joint with friction angle $\phi=30^\circ$ ($c=0$) under normal stress $\sigma_n=10\,\text{MPa}$, the shear strength is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock has cohesion $c=5\,\text{MPa}$ and friction angle $\phi=30^\circ$. The shear strength on a plane carrying normal stress $\sigma_n=20\,\text{MPa}$ is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

figure.caption: Mohr circle with the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope (c = 5 MPa, phi = 30 deg).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The coefficient of internal friction $\tan\phi$ for a friction angle of $35^\circ$ is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In the normal-stress versus shear-stress plane, the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope is
options: a straight line of slope $\tan\phi$ and intercept $c$
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\tau=c+\sigma_n\tan\phi$ is linear in $\sigma_n$.

**Working.** Slope $=\tan\phi$, intercept $=c$.

**Answer.** a straight line of slope $\tan\phi$ and intercept $c$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Using $\sigma_1=N\sigma_3+\sigma_c$ with $N=\tan^2(45^\circ+\phi/2)$, for $\phi=30^\circ$, $\sigma_c=30\,\text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_3=10\,\text{MPa}$, the axial stress at failure $\sigma_1$ is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The triaxial stress factor $N=\tan^2(45^\circ+\phi/2)$ for $\phi=30^\circ$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Two triaxial tests give $(\sigma_3,\sigma_1)=(5,35)$ and $(10,50)\,\text{MPa}$. The friction angle $\phi$ is _____ degrees.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: From the tests $(\sigma_3,\sigma_1)=(5,35)$ and $(10,50)\,\text{MPa}$ (slope $N=3$), the uniaxial compressive strength $\sigma_c$ (intercept) is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Given $\sigma_c=20\,\text{MPa}$ and $N=3$, the cohesion $c=\dfrac{\sigma_c}{2\sqrt{N}}$ is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The Mohr-Coulomb criterion is usually modified with a tension cutoff because it
options: overestimates the tensile strength of rock
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Extrapolating the linear envelope into tension predicts too large a tensile strength.

**Working.** A tension cutoff caps $\sigma_t$ at a realistic (small) value.

**Answer.** overestimates the tensile strength of rock.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Using $\sigma_t=\dfrac{2c\cos\phi}{1+\sin\phi}$ with $c=5.77\,\text{MPa}$ and $\phi=30^\circ$, the predicted tensile strength magnitude is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Griffith's theory predicts that the uniaxial compressive strength equals eight times the tensile strength. For $\sigma_t=5\,\text{MPa}$, the predicted compressive strength is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Griffith's theory of brittle failure attributes fracture to
options: stress concentration at the tips of pre-existing microcracks
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Griffith flaws act as stress raisers.

**Working.** Tensile stress concentrates at crack tips and drives propagation.

**Answer.** stress concentration at the tips of pre-existing microcracks.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For intact rock the Hoek-Brown parameter $s$ equals _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** For intact rock $s=1$; it decreases for jointed rock masses.

**Working.** $s=1$ for the undisturbed intact material.

**Answer.** $1$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The intact Hoek-Brown criterion is $\sigma_1=\sigma_3+\sigma_c\sqrt{m\,\sigma_3/\sigma_c+s}$. For $\sigma_c=100\,\text{MPa}$, $m=10$, $s=1$ and $\sigma_3=0$, $\sigma_1$ is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the intact Hoek-Brown criterion with $\sigma_c=100\,\text{MPa}$, $m=10$, $s=1$ and $\sigma_3=10\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_1$ is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In the Hoek-Brown criterion, the material constant $m_i$ depends primarily on
options: the rock type and its mineral texture
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $m_i$ is a curve-fitting constant characteristic of the intact rock.

**Working.** It is tabulated by rock type/texture (e.g. low for shale, high for granite).

**Answer.** the rock type and its mineral texture.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The shear strength available on a plane is $50\,\text{kPa}$ and the mobilised (driving) shear stress is $25\,\text{kPa}$. The factor of safety is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: On a plane with $c=10\,\text{kPa}$, $\phi=30^\circ$ and normal stress $\sigma_n=40\,\text{kPa}$, the driving shear stress is $30\,\text{kPa}$. The factor of safety is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: According to Mohr-Coulomb theory, the failure plane is inclined to the major principal stress direction by $45^\circ+\phi/2$. For $\phi=30^\circ$, this angle is _____ degrees.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a rock with $\phi=40^\circ$, the Mohr-Coulomb failure plane makes an angle of _____ degrees with the major principal plane direction ($45^\circ+\phi/2$).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In a triaxial compression test, increasing the confining pressure generally
options: increases the peak strength and ductility
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Confinement suppresses crack opening.

**Working.** Higher $\sigma_3$ raises peak strength and promotes ductile behaviour.

**Answer.** increases the peak strength and ductility.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In a triaxial test the axial stress at failure is $120\,\text{MPa}$ and the confining pressure is $30\,\text{MPa}$. The deviator stress at failure is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock has $\sigma_c=40\,\text{MPa}$ and tensile strength $\sigma_t=10\,\text{MPa}$. Using $c=\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\sigma_c\sigma_t}$, the cohesion is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: If the ratio $N=\sigma_c/\sigma_t=4$ and $N=\tan^2(45^\circ+\phi/2)$, the friction angle $\phi$ is _____ degrees. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The residual strength of a rock is
options: the strength sustained after the peak, on continued shearing
the strength at first crack initiation
always greater than the peak strength
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Beyond peak, strength drops to a residual plateau.

**Working.** It is the post-peak strength maintained during continued displacement.

**Answer.** the strength sustained after the peak, on continued shearing.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock has $c=2\,\text{MPa}$ and $\phi=25^\circ$. The shear strength on a plane with $\sigma_n=30\,\text{MPa}$ is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Which of the following are shear-strength parameters in the Mohr-Coulomb criterion? Select all that apply.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mohr-Coulomb: $\tau=c+\sigma_n\tan\phi$.

**Working.** Only $c$ and $\phi$ define the strength envelope; $E$ and $\nu$ are elastic (deformation) constants.

**Answer.** cohesion $c$ and friction angle $\phi$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock with a higher friction angle, all else equal, will have
options: a steeper failure envelope and higher shear strength at a given normal stress
no dependence of strength on normal stress
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Envelope slope is $\tan\phi$.

**Working.** Larger $\phi$ steepens the envelope, raising $\tau$ at any $\sigma_n$.

**Answer.** a steeper failure envelope and higher shear strength at a given normal stress.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Using $\sigma_1=N\sigma_3+\sigma_c$ with $N=4$ and $\sigma_c=25\,\text{MPa}$, the confining pressure $\sigma_3$ needed to reach $\sigma_1=85\,\text{MPa}$ is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In a core run of total length $100\,\text{cm}$, the sum of intact pieces at least $10\,\text{cm}$ long is $80\,\text{cm}$. The RQD is _____ percent.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: When computing RQD, the core pieces that are summed are those that are
options: intact and at least 100 mm long
questions.solution:
**Concept.** RQD counts only sound pieces $\ge 10\,\text{cm}$.

**Working.** Only intact lengths of at least 100 mm contribute.

**Answer.** intact and at least 100 mm long.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Using Palmstrom's relation $\text{RQD}=115-3.3\,J_v$ for a volumetric joint count $J_v=10\,\text{joints/m}^3$, the RQD is _____ percent.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock mass with RQD of $82\%$ is classified as
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Deere's RQD classes: 90-100 excellent, 75-90 good, 50-75 fair, 25-50 poor, <25 very poor.

**Working.** $82\%$ falls in 75-90.

**Answer.** good (75-90%).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Three joint sets have spacings $0.2\,\text{m}$, $0.5\,\text{m}$ and $1.0\,\text{m}$. The volumetric joint count $J_v=\sum(1/\text{spacing})$ is _____ joints/m$^3$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the joint network giving $J_v=8\,\text{joints/m}^3$, the estimated RQD ($=115-3.3J_v$) is _____ percent. Round to one decimal.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Bieniawski's RMR is obtained by summing ratings for a number of parameters and then adjusting for joint orientation. The number of rating parameters (before orientation adjustment) is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** RMR parameters: UCS, RQD, joint spacing, joint condition, groundwater (five), then orientation adjustment.

**Working.** Five basic ratings are summed.

**Answer.** five.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The five RMR ratings are: UCS $=7$, RQD $=17$, joint spacing $=10$, joint condition $=20$, groundwater $=10$, and the orientation adjustment is $-5$. The RMR is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock mass with RMR $=59$ falls in
questions.solution:
**Concept.** RMR classes in steps of 20: I (81-100), II (61-80), III (41-60), IV (21-40), V (<21).

**Working.** $59$ lies in 41-60.

**Answer.** Class III — fair rock.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Barton's $Q=\dfrac{\text{RQD}}{J_n}\cdot\dfrac{J_r}{J_a}\cdot\dfrac{J_w}{\text{SRF}}$. For RQD $=90$, $J_n=9$, $J_r=3$, $J_a=1$, $J_w=1$, SRF $=1$, the value of $Q$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For RQD $=75$, $J_n=6$, $J_r=2$, $J_a=2$, $J_w=0.66$, SRF $=2.5$, Barton's $Q$ is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In Barton's Q-system, the parameter $J_n$ represents
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $J_n$ = joint set number.

**Working.** It quantifies how many joint sets cut the rock mass.

**Answer.** the number of joint sets.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In the Q-system, SRF stands for
questions.solution:
**Concept.** SRF accounts for in-situ stress and loosening loads.

**Working.** It is the Stress Reduction Factor.

**Answer.** Stress Reduction Factor.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Using the approximation $\text{GSI}\approx\text{RMR}_{89}-5$ (for RMR $>23$), the GSI for a rock mass with RMR $=59$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Using Bieniawski's correlation $\text{RMR}=9\ln Q+44$, the RMR corresponding to $Q=30$ is _____ . Round to one decimal.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The Geological Strength Index (GSI) is primarily used as input to
questions.solution:
**Concept.** GSI scales the Hoek-Brown constants $m_b$, $s$, $a$.

**Working.** It links field observations to rock-mass strength.

**Answer.** the Hoek-Brown rock-mass strength criterion.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A $200\,\text{cm}$ core run contains intact pieces $\ge 10\,\text{cm}$ summing to $150\,\text{cm}$. The RQD is _____ percent.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A higher RQD value generally indicates
options: a more massive, less fractured rock mass
questions.solution:
**Concept.** RQD measures the percentage of sound long core pieces.

**Working.** Higher RQD means fewer fractures and better quality.

**Answer.** a more massive, less fractured rock mass.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a very blocky mass with $J_v=4.5\,\text{joints/m}^3$, $\text{RQD}=115-3.3J_v$ gives $100.15$, which is reported as a capped value of _____ percent.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** RQD cannot exceed 100%, so values above 100 are capped.

**Working.**
$$115-3.3(4.5)=100.15\rightarrow 100\%.$$

**Answer.** $100\%$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In Barton's Q-system, the ratio $J_r/J_a$ broadly represents
options: the shear strength (roughness vs alteration) of the joints
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $J_r$ (roughness) over $J_a$ (alteration) reflects inter-block frictional strength.

**Working.** Rougher, unaltered joints give higher $J_r/J_a$ and more shear strength.

**Answer.** the shear strength of the joints.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In a room-and-pillar layout, $300\,\text{m}^2$ out of every $400\,\text{m}^2$ of plan area is mined out. The extraction ratio is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Square pillars of width $10\,\text{m}$ are separated by $10\,\text{m}$ wide openings. With vertical stress $\sigma_v=10\,\text{MPa}$, the tributary-area pillar stress $\sigma_p=\sigma_v\left(\dfrac{W_p+W_o}{W_p}\right)^2$ is _____ MPa.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 200' width='200' height='200'><rect x='10' y='10' width='180' height='180' fill='#f8fafc' stroke='#94a3b8'/><rect x='30' y='30' width='40' height='40' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='110' y='30' width='40' height='40' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='30' y='110' width='40' height='40' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='110' y='110' width='40' height='40' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='30' y='30' width='120' height='120' fill='none' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><text x='38' y='54' font-size='9' fill='#475569'>pillar</text><text x='95' y='185' font-size='9' fill='#1d4ed8'>tributary area</text></svg>
figure.caption: Plan of square pillars (grey) with the tributary area (dashed) of one pillar.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Using $\sigma_p=\dfrac{\sigma_v}{1-e}$ with $\sigma_v=10\,\text{MPa}$ and extraction ratio $e=0.75$, the pillar stress is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Tributary-area theory for pillar design assumes that
options: each pillar carries the full overburden weight over its share of the area
the roof beam supports all the load
stress is uniform throughout the rock mass
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Load over the opening is transferred to adjacent pillars.

**Working.** Each pillar supports the overburden above its tributary area.

**Answer.** each pillar carries the full overburden weight over its share of the area.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A pillar has strength $80\,\text{MPa}$ and carries a stress of $40\,\text{MPa}$. The factor of safety is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The Obert-Duvall pillar strength is $S_p=S_1\left(0.778+0.222\dfrac{W_p}{H_p}\right)$. For $S_1=60\,\text{MPa}$ and $W_p/H_p=2$, $S_p$ is _____ MPa. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A pillar is $4\,\text{m}$ wide and $2\,\text{m}$ high. Its width-to-height ratio is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A circular opening sits in a hydrostatic stress field $p_0=15\,\text{MPa}$. By the Kirsch solution, the tangential stress at the opening boundary is uniform and equals _____ MPa.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 200' width='200' height='200'><rect x='5' y='5' width='190' height='190' fill='#f8fafc' stroke='#94a3b8'/><circle cx='100' cy='100' r='45' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='15' x2='100' y2='50' stroke='#b91c1c' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='185' x2='100' y2='150' stroke='#b91c1c' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='15' y1='100' x2='50' y2='100' stroke='#b91c1c' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='185' y1='100' x2='150' y2='100' stroke='#b91c1c' stroke-width='2'/><text x='70' y='100' font-size='10' fill='#475569'>opening</text></svg>
figure.caption: Circular opening in a hydrostatic far-field stress (arrows).

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For a hydrostatic field, $\sigma_\theta=2p_0$ everywhere on the boundary.

**Working.**
$$\sigma_\theta=2\times15=30.$$

**Answer.** $30\,\text{MPa}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a circular opening with vertical field stress $\sigma_v=10\,\text{MPa}$ and horizontal stress $\sigma_h=0$, the tangential stress at the sidewall (springline) is $3\sigma_v-\sigma_h$, i.e. _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the same opening ($\sigma_v=10$, $\sigma_h=0\,\text{MPa}$), the tangential stress at the crown (roof) is $3\sigma_h-\sigma_v$, i.e. _____ MPa (a negative value indicates tension).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a circular hole in a plate under uniaxial stress, the maximum boundary stress-concentration factor is
options: 3, at the points on the diameter perpendicular to the applied stress
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Classic Kirsch result for uniaxial loading.

**Working.** $\sigma_\theta=3\sigma$ at $\theta=90^\circ$ to the load.

**Answer.** 3, at the points perpendicular to the applied stress.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A uniaxial field stress of $20\,\text{MPa}$ acts around a circular opening. The maximum tangential boundary stress is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A circular opening shape is often preferred over a rectangular one because it
options: produces lower and smoother stress concentrations than sharp corners
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sharp corners concentrate stress severely.

**Working.** A circle distributes boundary stress smoothly, avoiding corner peaks.

**Answer.** produces lower and smoother stress concentrations than sharp corners.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Pillar stress is $50\,\text{MPa}$ when the vertical field stress is $10\,\text{MPa}$. Using $\sigma_p=\sigma_v/(1-e)$, the extraction ratio $e$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: With vertical stress $\sigma_v=12.5\,\text{MPa}$ and extraction ratio $e=0.6$, the tributary-area pillar stress is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: As the extraction ratio of a room-and-pillar panel increases, the pillar stress

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Square pillars $8\,\text{m}$ wide are separated by $4\,\text{m}$ openings under $\sigma_v=20\,\text{MPa}$. The pillar stress $\sigma_v\left(\dfrac{W_p+W_o}{W_p}\right)^2$ is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a long rib pillar, $\sigma_p=\sigma_v\dfrac{W_p+W_o}{W_p}$. With $W_p=8\,\text{m}$, $W_o=4\,\text{m}$ and $\sigma_v=20\,\text{MPa}$, the pillar stress is _____ MPa.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rib pillars use the linear (not squared) width ratio.

**Working.**
$$\sigma_p=20\times\frac{12}{8}=20(1.5)=30.$$

**Answer.** $30\,\text{MPa}$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Compared with a long rib pillar, the tributary-area stress formula for a square pillar uses
options: the square of the width ratio instead of the linear ratio
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Square pillars draw load from two horizontal directions.

**Working.** Hence $\left(\tfrac{W_p+W_o}{W_p}\right)^2$ versus the linear ratio for rib pillars.

**Answer.** the square of the width ratio instead of the linear ratio.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A pillar with Obert-Duvall strength $73.32\,\text{MPa}$ carries a stress of $40\,\text{MPa}$. The factor of safety is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a dry cohesionless plane failure, $\text{FoS}=\dfrac{\tan\phi}{\tan\psi_p}$. With $\phi=30^\circ$ and discontinuity dip $\psi_p=30^\circ$, the factor of safety is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For plane (planar) sliding to be kinematically possible, the discontinuity must
options: daylight in the slope face and dip less steeply than the face but steeper than $\phi$
dip steeper than the slope face
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Daylighting and the friction condition govern feasibility.

**Working.** The plane must dip out of the face ($\psi_p<\psi_f$) and exceed friction ($\psi_p>\phi$).

**Answer.** daylight in the slope face and dip less steeply than the face but steeper than $\phi$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a dry cohesionless plane with $\phi=40^\circ$ sliding on a plane dipping $\psi_p=35^\circ$, the factor of safety $\tan\phi/\tan\psi_p$ is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A dry cohesionless infinite slope has $\phi=38^\circ$ and slope angle $\beta=25^\circ$. The factor of safety $\tan\phi/\tan\beta$ is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A computed factor of safety of exactly $1.0$ for a slope indicates
questions.solution:
**Concept.** FoS is resisting over driving forces.

**Working.** $\text{FoS}=1$ means they are equal — the verge of failure.

**Answer.** limiting equilibrium.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a dry cohesionless slope, the maximum stable (critical) slope angle equals the friction angle. For $\phi=33^\circ$, the critical slope angle is _____ degrees.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\beta_{crit}=\phi$ when $c=0$ and the slope is dry.

**Working.** At $\beta=\phi$, $\text{FoS}=1$.

**Answer.** $33^\circ$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A planar block has weight $W=2000\,\text{kN}$ on a plane dipping $\psi_p=35^\circ$, with base area $A=10\,\text{m}^2$, cohesion $c=20\,\text{kPa}$ and $\phi=30^\circ$ (dry). Using $\text{FoS}=\dfrac{cA+W\cos\psi_p\tan\phi}{W\sin\psi_p}$, the factor of safety is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Wedge failure of a rock slope involves sliding
options: along the line of intersection of two discontinuity planes
along a single horizontal bedding plane
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Two intersecting planes form a wedge.

**Working.** The wedge slides along their line of intersection.

**Answer.** along the line of intersection of two discontinuity planes.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Kinematic analysis of rock-slope failure modes is most commonly carried out using
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Orientations of planes and slope are plotted as poles/great circles.

**Working.** Stereonets reveal daylighting and friction-cone conditions.

**Answer.** stereographic projection (stereonets).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a fully saturated cohesionless infinite slope with seepage parallel to the surface, $\text{FoS}=\dfrac{\gamma'}{\gamma_{sat}}\dfrac{\tan\phi}{\tan\beta}$. With $\gamma'=11$, $\gamma_{sat}=20\,\text{kN/m}^3$, $\phi=35^\circ$, $\beta=25^\circ$, the FoS is _____ . Round to two decimals.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Saturation reduces FoS by the factor $\gamma'/\gamma_{sat}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{FoS}=\frac{11}{20}\cdot\frac{\tan35^\circ}{\tan25^\circ}=0.55\times1.502=0.83.$$

**Answer.** $\approx 0.83$ (unstable).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A slope face dips $45^\circ$ and a joint dips $30^\circ$ in the same direction; the friction angle is $20^\circ$. For this joint, plane sliding is

figure.caption: Stereonet: slope face (dip 45 deg) and a joint (dip 30 deg) in the same direction.

options: kinematically feasible (joint daylights and dips steeper than $\phi$)
impossible because the joint dips into the slope
impossible because the joint is flatter than $\phi$
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Need $\phi<\psi_p<\psi_f$.

**Working.** Here $20^\circ<30^\circ<45^\circ$, so the joint daylights and exceeds friction.

**Answer.** kinematically feasible.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The minimum number of discontinuity planes that must intersect to define a rock wedge for wedge sliding is _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A wedge is bounded by two intersecting planes (plus the face).

**Working.** Their intersection forms the sliding line.

**Answer.** $2$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A water-filled tension crack at the crest of a slope generally
options: reduces stability by adding a driving water thrust
has no effect on the factor of safety
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Water pressure in the crack pushes the block outward.

**Working.** It adds a destabilising force, lowering FoS.

**Answer.** reduces stability by adding a driving water thrust.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For a circular slip surface, the resisting moment is $5000\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$ and the driving moment is $2500\,\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}$. The factor of safety is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For most rock slopes, the least favourable (most critical) groundwater condition is
options: fully saturated with high pore-water pressure
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Pore pressure reduces effective normal stress and shear strength.

**Working.** Full saturation gives the highest uplift/water thrust and lowest FoS.

**Answer.** fully saturated with high pore-water pressure.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The maximum surface subsidence over a critical-width extraction is $S=a\cdot m$, where $a$ is the subsidence factor and $m$ the extracted seam thickness. For $a=0.6$ and $m=2\,\text{m}$, the maximum subsidence is _____ m.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The angle of draw in subsidence engineering is
options: the angle from the vertical between the panel edge and the limit of surface subsidence
the slope of the subsidence trough at its steepest point
the friction angle of the overburden
questions.solution:
**Concept.** It defines the lateral extent of the subsidence basin.

**Working.** Measured from vertical at the working edge to the trough limit on surface.

**Answer.** the angle from the vertical between the panel edge and the limit of surface subsidence.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: With overburden depth $H=200\,\text{m}$ and angle of draw $25^\circ$, the horizontal distance the subsidence trough extends beyond the panel edge ($H\tan 25^\circ$) is _____ m. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The critical width of extraction is the width at which
options: the maximum possible subsidence is first achieved at a surface point
the angle of draw becomes zero
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Below critical width, subsidence is sub-maximal (subcritical).

**Working.** At critical width the centre of the basin first reaches $S_{max}$.

**Answer.** the maximum possible subsidence is first achieved.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rockburst is best described as
options: a sudden, violent failure releasing stored elastic strain energy
questions.solution:
**Concept.** High stored strain energy in brittle, highly stressed rock.

**Working.** Its sudden release ejects rock violently.

**Answer.** a sudden, violent failure releasing stored elastic strain energy.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The elastic strain-energy density at failure is $U=\dfrac{\sigma^2}{2E}$. For $\sigma=100\,\text{MPa}$ and $E=50\,\text{GPa}$, $U$ in kJ/m$^3$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Convergence (closure) between the walls of an underground opening is most directly measured with a
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Convergence is a relative displacement of opposing surfaces.

**Working.** A convergence meter or tape extensometer records this closure.

**Answer.** convergence meter / tape extensometer.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The flat-jack test is used primarily to determine
options: the in-situ stress in a rock wall
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A slot is cut, relieving stress; a flat jack is pressurised to restore the original opening.

**Working.** The cancellation pressure equals the in-situ stress normal to the slot.

**Answer.** the in-situ stress in a rock wall.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Overcoring with a strain cell is a technique used to measure
options: the in-situ stress state in rock
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stress relief on overcoring lets a recovered core expand.

**Working.** Measured relief strains are converted to the in-situ stress tensor.

**Answer.** the in-situ stress state in rock.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A tunnel of initial diameter $5.00\,\text{m}$ closes to $4.92\,\text{m}$. The convergence is _____ mm.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the stress state shown by the Mohr circle ($\sigma_1=80$, $\sigma_3=20\ \mathrm{MPa}$), the centre of the circle (mean normal stress) is _____ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the Mohr circle shown ($\sigma_1=80$, $\sigma_3=20\ \mathrm{MPa}$), the maximum shear stress (radius), in MPa, is _____.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.topic: Mohr circle — plane of maximum shear
questions.stem: For the Mohr circle shown ($\sigma_1=60$, $\sigma_3=20\ \mathrm{MPa}$), the normal stress acting on the plane of maximum shear, in MPa, is _____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** On the plane of maximum shear the normal stress equals the centre of the circle.

**Working.**
$$\sigma_n=\frac{60+20}{2}=40\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer.** $40$ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock has an angle of internal friction $\phi=30^\circ$ (see Mohr–Coulomb diagram). The failure plane makes an angle of _____ degrees with the direction of the major principal stress $\sigma_1$.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The failure plane is inclined at $\theta=45^\circ+\dfrac{\phi}{2}$ to $\sigma_1$.

**Working.**
$$\theta=45^\circ+\frac{30^\circ}{2}=60^\circ$$

**Answer.** $60^\circ$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the stress element shown ($\sigma_x=40$, $\sigma_y=20$, $\tau_{xy}=10\ \mathrm{MPa}$), the major principal stress, in MPa, is _____ (two decimals).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the stress element shown ($\sigma_x=40$, $\sigma_y=20$, $\tau_{xy}=10\ \mathrm{MPa}$), the maximum in-plane shear stress, in MPa, is _____ (two decimals).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the stress element shown ($\sigma_x=30$, $\sigma_y=10$, $\tau_{xy}=10\ \mathrm{MPa}$), the angle $\theta_p$ of the major principal plane to the x-axis, in degrees, is _____ (one decimal).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Two planes are plotted on the equal-area stereonet shown: plane A (strike 000, dip 30) and plane B (strike 090, dip 60). The plane with the steeper dip is
options: plane B, because a 60-degree dip is steeper than a 30-degree dip
cannot be determined from a stereonet
questions.solution:
**Concept.** On a stereonet a steeper plane plots as a great circle closer to the centre.

**Reasoning.** Plane B dips at 60 degrees versus 30 degrees for A, so B is steeper and its great circle lies nearer the centre.

**Answer.** Plane B.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: From the linear portion of the stress–strain curve shown, a stress of 50 MPa corresponds to a strain of 0.001. The Young's modulus, in GPa, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='150' x2='220' y2='150' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='150' x2='40' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='150' x2='180' y2='50' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='180' y1='150' x2='180' y2='50' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='40' y1='50' x2='180' y2='50' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='150' y='44' font-size='9'>50 MPa</text><text x='140' y='168' font-size='9'>strain 0.001</text><text x='8' y='90' font-size='9'>stress</text></svg>

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The complete stress–strain curve shown peaks at a stress of 80 MPa before failure. The uniaxial compressive strength of the rock, in MPa, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='150' x2='220' y2='150' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='150' x2='40' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M40 150 Q110 60 150 50 L165 80' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='150' cy='50' r='3' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='148' y='44' font-size='9'>peak 80 MPa</text><text x='90' y='168' font-size='9'>strain</text><text x='8' y='90' font-size='9'>stress</text></svg>
figure.caption: Complete stress–strain curve

questions.solution:
**Concept.** UCS is the peak axial stress under uniaxial loading.

**Reasoning.** The maximum ordinate of the curve is 80 MPa, which is the compressive strength.

**Answer.** $80$ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In the triaxial test shown, a cohesionless rock has $\phi=30^\circ$ and is subjected to a confining pressure $\sigma_3=10\ \mathrm{MPa}$. Using $\sigma_1=\sigma_3\tan^2(45^\circ+\phi/2)$, the major principal stress at failure, in MPa, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='70' y='50' width='40' height='90' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><g stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'><line x1='80' y1='25' x2='80' y2='48' marker-end='url(#t1)'/><line x1='100' y1='25' x2='100' y2='48' marker-end='url(#t1)'/></g><g stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'><line x1='40' y1='95' x2='66' y2='95' marker-end='url(#t2)'/><line x1='140' y1='95' x2='114' y2='95' marker-end='url(#t2)'/></g><defs><marker id='t1' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker><marker id='t2' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='104' y='35' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>s1</text><text x='6' y='90' font-size='9' fill='#2563eb'>s3=10</text></svg>
figure.caption: Triaxial test specimen

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In the Brazilian test shown, a disc of diameter $D=50\ \mathrm{mm}$ and thickness $L=50\ \mathrm{mm}$ fails at a load $P=30\ \mathrm{kN}$. Using $\sigma_t=\dfrac{2P}{\pi D L}$, the tensile strength, in MPa, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='90' cy='95' r='45' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='45' x2='140' y2='45' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='40' y1='145' x2='140' y2='145' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='90' y1='25' x2='90' y2='42' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#bz)'/><defs><marker id='bz' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='96' y='20' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>P = 30 kN</text><text x='52' y='170' font-size='9'>D=50 mm, L=50 mm</text></svg>
figure.caption: Brazilian disc under diametral load

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In the point-load test shown, a specimen of equivalent core diameter $D_e=50\ \mathrm{mm}$ fails at $P=10\ \mathrm{kN}$. The uncorrected point-load index $I_s=P/D_e^2$, in MPa, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='60' y='70' width='60' height='40' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M85 50 L95 50 L90 70 Z' fill='#1f2937'/><path d='M85 130 L95 130 L90 110 Z' fill='#1f2937'/><line x1='90' y1='25' x2='90' y2='46' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#pl)'/><defs><marker id='pl' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='96' y='20' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>P = 10 kN</text><text x='58' y='168' font-size='9'>De = 50 mm</text></svg>
figure.caption: Point-load test

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the cantilever beam shown, a point load $W=10\ \mathrm{kN}$ acts at the free end, $L=3\ \mathrm{m}$ from the fixed support. The maximum bending moment (at the support), in kN·m, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='40' width='12' height='80' fill='#475569'/><line x1='32' y1='60' x2='200' y2='60' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='200' y1='25' x2='200' y2='56' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#cl)'/><defs><marker id='cl' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='176' y='20' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>W = 10 kN</text><text x='90' y='80' font-size='9'>L = 3 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cantilever with end load

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For a tip-loaded cantilever, $M_{max}=W\,L$ at the fixed end.

**Working.**
$$M_{max}=10\times3=30\ \text{kN·m}$$

**Answer.** $30$ kN·m.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A simply supported beam of span $L=4\ \mathrm{m}$ carries a central point load $W=20\ \mathrm{kN}$ (see figure). The maximum bending moment, in kN·m, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='80' x2='210' y2='80' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M30 80 L22 95 L38 95 Z' fill='#475569'/><path d='M210 80 L202 95 L218 95 Z' fill='#475569'/><line x1='120' y1='40' x2='120' y2='76' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#sp)'/><defs><marker id='sp' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='100' y='32' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>W = 20 kN</text><text x='100' y='112' font-size='9'>L = 4 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Simply supported beam, central load

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A simply supported beam of span $L=4\ \mathrm{m}$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w=10\ \mathrm{kN/m}$ (see figure). The maximum bending moment, in kN·m, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='80' x2='210' y2='80' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M30 80 L22 95 L38 95 Z' fill='#475569'/><path d='M210 80 L202 95 L218 95 Z' fill='#475569'/><g stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'><line x1='40' y1='45' x2='40' y2='74' marker-end='url(#ud)'/><line x1='73' y1='45' x2='73' y2='74' marker-end='url(#ud)'/><line x1='106' y1='45' x2='106' y2='74' marker-end='url(#ud)'/><line x1='139' y1='45' x2='139' y2='74' marker-end='url(#ud)'/><line x1='172' y1='45' x2='172' y2='74' marker-end='url(#ud)'/><line x1='200' y1='45' x2='200' y2='74' marker-end='url(#ud)'/></g><defs><marker id='ud' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='78' y='38' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>w = 10 kN/m</text><text x='100' y='112' font-size='9'>L = 4 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Simply supported beam, UDL

questions.solution:
**Concept.** UDL on a simple span: $M_{max}=\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$.

**Working.**
$$M_{max}=\frac{10\times4^2}{8}=\frac{160}{8}=20\ \text{kN·m}$$

**Answer.** $20$ kN·m.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A simply supported beam carries a single point load of $12\ \mathrm{kN}$ at mid-span (see figure). By symmetry, the reaction at each support, in kN, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='80' x2='210' y2='80' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M30 80 L22 95 L38 95 Z' fill='#475569'/><path d='M210 80 L202 95 L218 95 Z' fill='#475569'/><line x1='120' y1='44' x2='120' y2='76' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#rp)'/><defs><marker id='rp' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='98' y='36' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>12 kN at mid-span</text><text x='20' y='112' font-size='9'>RA</text><text x='198' y='112' font-size='9'>RB</text></svg>
figure.caption: Symmetric simply supported beam

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For a symmetric load, each reaction carries half the load.

**Working.**
$$R_A=R_B=\frac{12}{2}=6\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** $6$ kN.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A block (see figure) is on the point of sliding down a rough plane inclined at $30^\circ$. The coefficient of static friction is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M20 140 L200 140 L200 60 Z' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='120' y='90' width='28' height='20' fill='#b45309' transform='rotate(-24 134 100)'/><path d='M170 140 A40 40 0 0 0 158 113' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626'/><text x='150' y='134' font-size='10' fill='#dc2626'>30°</text><text x='52' y='128' font-size='9'>block on verge of sliding</text></svg>
figure.caption: Block on a rough incline

questions.solution:
**Concept.** At the verge of sliding, $\mu=\tan\theta$ (angle of repose).

**Working.**
$$\mu=\tan 30^\circ=0.577\approx0.58$$

**Answer.** $0.58$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A block of mass $10\ \mathrm{kg}$ rests on a horizontal surface with coefficient of friction $\mu=0.3$ (see figure). The maximum friction force that can be developed, in N, is _____ (two decimals; take $g=9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$).

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For the dry cohesionless infinite slope shown, the slope angle is $\beta=20^\circ$ and the friction angle is $\phi=30^\circ$. The factor of safety ($\tan\phi/\tan\beta$) is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M20 130 L210 60' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='3' fill='none'/><line x1='20' y1='130' x2='210' y2='130' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M170 130 A50 50 0 0 0 162 110' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626'/><text x='150' y='124' font-size='10' fill='#dc2626'>beta=20°</text><text x='56' y='95' font-size='9'>phi = 30°</text></svg>
figure.caption: Dry cohesionless slope

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For a dry cohesionless infinite slope, $FoS=\dfrac{\tan\phi}{\tan\beta}$.

**Working.**
$$FoS=\frac{\tan 30^\circ}{\tan 20^\circ}=\frac{0.5774}{0.3640}=1.59$$

**Answer.** $1.59$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The rock block in the figure rests on a daylighting discontinuity (dashed). Sliding occurs when

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M20 130 L150 40 L210 130 Z' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><line x1='150' y1='40' x2='95' y2='130' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5 3'/><text x='46' y='120' font-size='9'>sliding block</text><text x='118' y='95' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>failure plane</text></svg>
figure.caption: Planar slope failure

options: the resisting force exceeds the driving force
the driving (gravity) component along the plane exceeds the available shear resistance
the discontinuity dips into the slope
the block is fully submerged only
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Limit equilibrium compares driving and resisting forces.

**Reasoning.** Failure (FoS < 1) occurs when the downslope gravity component on the plane exceeds the shear resistance (friction plus cohesion). A plane that daylights and dips out of the slope is a prerequisite.

**Answer.** Driving force exceeds shear resistance.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='70' y='70' width='50' height='50' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><g stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'><line x1='95' y1='35' x2='95' y2='66' marker-end='url(#kv)'/><line x1='95' y1='155' x2='95' y2='124' marker-end='url(#kv)'/></g><g stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'><line x1='35' y1='95' x2='66' y2='95' marker-end='url(#kh)'/><line x1='155' y1='95' x2='124' y2='95' marker-end='url(#kh)'/></g><defs><marker id='kv' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker><marker id='kh' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='100' y='30' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>sv=10</text><text x='6' y='90' font-size='9' fill='#2563eb'>sh=6</text></svg>

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: In the flat-jack method illustrated, a slot is cut in the rock face and a flat jack is grouted in. The jack pressure required to restore the slot to its original width gives a measure of

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='20' width='180' height='110' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#78716c'/><line x1='60' y1='40' x2='160' y2='40' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='95' y='36' width='30' height='8' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='66' y='70' font-size='9'>slot + flat jack</text></svg>
figure.caption: Flat-jack installation

options: the in-situ stress normal to the slot (the cancellation pressure)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cutting the slot relaxes the rock; the jack restores it.

**Reasoning.** The pressure that exactly cancels the slot closure (cancellation pressure) equals the in-situ stress acting normal to the slot.

**Answer.** In-situ stress normal to the slot.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A fully grouted rock bolt (see figure) has diameter $d=25\ \mathrm{mm}$, anchored length $L=2\ \mathrm{m}$, and a grout–rock bond strength $\tau=1\ \mathrm{MPa}$. The pull-out capacity ($\pi d L \tau$), in kN, is _____ (nearest integer).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='20' width='140' height='150' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#78716c'/><line x1='90' y1='30' x2='90' y2='150' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='4'/><rect x='78' y='150' width='24' height='8' fill='#475569'/><line x1='90' y1='158' x2='90' y2='185' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#bo)'/><defs><marker id='bo' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='0' orient='auto'><path d='M0 6 L4 0 L8 6 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='96' y='90' font-size='9'>L=2 m, d=25 mm</text><text x='96' y='182' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>pull-out</text></svg>
figure.caption: Grouted rock bolt

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Pull-out capacity $=\tau\times$ bond surface area $=\tau\,\pi d L$.

**Working.**
$$P=1\times10^{6}\times\pi\times0.025\times2=157080\ \text{N}\approx157\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** $157$ kN.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock element is subjected to principal stresses $\sigma_1=80\ \text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_3=20\ \text{MPa}$. From the Mohr circle, compute the maximum shear stress (MPa).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Maximum shear stress equals the radius of the Mohr circle, $\tau_{max}=\dfrac{\sigma_1-\sigma_3}{2}$.

**Working.**
$$\tau_{max}=\frac{80-20}{2}=30\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer.** $30$ MPa.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: On the equal-area stereonet shown, a discontinuity plane is represented by:
options: a single point at the centre
a small circle of fixed radius
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stereographic projection of planes and lines.

**Reasoning.** A plane projects as a great circle (its cyclographic trace), while its pole projects as a point; lines project as points. Hence the discontinuity plane appears as a great circle.

**Answer.** A great circle.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: According to the Obert-Duvall (and similar) pillar-strength formulae, for a given rock the pillar strength:

figure.caption: Pillar width-to-height
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='70' y='40' width='60' height='70' fill='#78716c' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='70' y1='125' x2='130' y2='125' stroke='#475569' marker-end='url(#wh)' marker-start='url(#wh)'/><line x1='150' y1='40' x2='150' y2='110' stroke='#475569' marker-end='url(#wh)' marker-start='url(#wh)'/><defs><marker id='wh' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='3' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='95' y='138' font-size='8'>w</text><text x='158' y='80' font-size='8'>h</text></svg>

options: increases as the width-to-height ratio increases
decreases as the width-to-height ratio increases
is independent of the width-to-height ratio
depends only on the depth of cover
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Pillar-strength formulae include a width-to-height term.

**Reasoning.** Squat pillars (large $w/h$) are confined and stronger, so strength rises with increasing width-to-height ratio; slender pillars are weaker.

**Answer.** Increases as the width-to-height ratio increases.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The bar chart shows the parameter ratings summed to obtain a rock-mass rating (RMR). A higher total RMR indicates:

figure.caption: RMR parameter ratings
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='100' x2='220' y2='100' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'><rect x='40' y='70' width='25' height='30'/><rect x='75' y='55' width='25' height='45'/><rect x='110' y='40' width='25' height='60'/><rect x='145' y='60' width='25' height='40'/><rect x='180' y='80' width='25' height='20'/></g><text x='45' y='120' font-size='8'>RMR parameter ratings</text></svg>

options: a better-quality, more stable rock mass
a weaker, more unstable rock mass
a rock mass that always requires heavy support
questions.solution:
**Concept.** RMR sums ratings for strength, RQD, spacing, joint condition and groundwater.

**Reasoning.** Larger ratings reflect stronger rock, better core recovery and favourable joints, so a higher total RMR denotes a better-quality, more stable rock mass needing lighter support.

**Answer.** A better-quality, more stable rock mass.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Two joint sets intersect to form a removable block that daylights in a rock slope, as shown. The most likely failure mode is:

figure.caption: Intersecting joints forming a block
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,120 160,120 100,40' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='100' y1='40' x2='70' y2='120' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='40' x2='130' y2='120' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='50' y='140' font-size='8'>two joints form a wedge</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Structurally controlled slope failures depend on discontinuity geometry.

**Reasoning.** When two discontinuities intersect and their line of intersection daylights in the slope face below the friction angle, the bounded block slides out as a wedge — wedge failure.

**Answer.** Wedge failure.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The stereonet shows a discontinuity whose dip direction matches the slope face and dips less steeply than it (figure). The likely failure mode is:
options: planar sliding, since the discontinuity daylights in the face and dips flatter than it
toppling, since the joint dips steeply into the slope
circular failure through intact rock only
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Kinematic analysis of slopes.

**Reasoning.** When a discontinuity dips in the same direction as the slope face but at a flatter angle, it daylights in the face and the block above can slide along it: planar sliding.

**Answer.** Planar sliding, since the discontinuity daylights in the face and dips flatter than it.
